Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. With 16 online courses and working to make ends meet on limited resources, I couldn't afford to be super structured. I'd usually try to squeeze in study time on weekends or late evenings after everything else was done. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically do things to understand them, so I'd often print materials when I could afford it and work through problems by hand. About seven hours a week was all I could manage consistently.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, but then something would come up—bills, family stuff, just life being overwhelming—and I'd have to cram everything near deadlines. At 29, I have responsibilities that younger students might not have, so I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. The anxiety of falling behind was constant, but I did my best to show up when it mattered.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting an email notification that something new was posted. I'd also log in when I felt that familiar wave of anxiety about falling behind—like a guilt check to see what I was missing. Sometimes I'd log in during my lunch break just to feel like I was staying connected to the course. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped me recognize when I was logging in out of panic versus genuine study intention.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking my phone. I'd scroll through announcements and see what discussions were happening. It became routine because the anxiety of not knowing what was due was worse than just checking regularly. I wouldn't say it was a comfortable routine—more like a necessary habit to manage my stress levels.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful because I could see how other students approached problems, and that hands-on exchange felt more real to me. Any interactive elements or practice exercises were gold for my learning style—I need to do things, not just read about them. Videos were okay but only when I could pause and try things myself. The peer interactions, even just 20 of them, made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials and theoretical documents. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would directly help me pass. Some supplementary videos I'd ignore because they took too long and my internet isn't always reliable. If something didn't seem immediately relevant to an assignment or exam, it went to the bottom of my list. I know that probably hurt my deeper understanding, but survival mode doesn't allow for extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or if it involved actually doing something rather than passive consumption. When I could practice a skill or apply a concept, that's when I felt like I was really learning. Time is precious when you're juggling everything, so anything that felt like busywork got pushed aside. The practical, hands-on stuff always won out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work got my attention because it directly affected my grade, and I couldn't risk failing. With optional stuff, there was always this guilt of knowing I should do it but also knowing I didn't have the bandwidth. Sometimes I'd look at optional materials briefly but couldn't commit the mental energy to really engage with them. The pressure of required work was stressful, but at least it gave me clear priorities.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download PDFs and work through them on paper because that's how I learn best—physically writing, highlighting, making notes in margins. I'd use my phone to look up concepts on YouTube when the course videos weren't clicking for me. Sometimes I'd study with a friend from another program who could explain things differently. A lot of my real learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd print something once and then spend hours studying it at the kitchen table or on my commute. The LMS would just show one download, but that paper might have gotten three or four study sessions. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal and self-testing that obviously wouldn't show up anywhere. Group study sessions with peers happened in person, completely invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or readings, then work through them over several days without going back online. My internet can be unreliable, so having offline copies was essential. I'd annotate them, create my own practice problems, revisit them multiple times. The LMS would show one access, but the actual engagement was much deeper and longer than that single click suggests.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike, and sometimes I'd freeze instead of being productive. Financial stress made it hard to focus—worrying about bills doesn't leave much mental space for coursework. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but my resilience is pretty low, so setbacks hit me hard. Some weeks I just went through the motions.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, the LMS became something I'd dread opening. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate because starting felt impossible. Fatigue meant I'd log in, stare at the screen, and absorb nothing. My engagement was directly tied to how much bandwidth I had left after dealing with everything else in my life.
Q14: Yes, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, replaying stressful conversations, just zoning out. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times without retaining anything. The LMS would log my time on the page, but my brain wasn't really there. High anxiety makes it really hard to be present even when you're technically doing the work.
Q15: I postponed assignments regularly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety of potentially failing made starting feel overwhelming—like if I didn't start, I couldn't fail yet. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because I felt like I had nothing smart to contribute. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this avoidance pattern. It's frustrating because I knew better, but knowing and doing are different things when anxiety is running the show.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd kick into high gear in the days before something was due, which is why my login frequency stayed decent. Without a deadline, I'd drift and let things slide. The pressure was stressful but necessary—I needed that external structure because my internal motivation was so low. My best work and worst anxiety both happened in deadline crunches.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, sometimes too much. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but critical feedback could send me spiraling for days. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my limited study time better. But emotionally, the anticipation of feedback was often worse than the feedback itself.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework I desperately needed. Clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize when everything felt overwhelming. Having 16 courses meant I relied heavily on whatever structure each course provided. When things were well-organized, I could function despite my low motivation. Unclear expectations made my anxiety worse and my engagement drop.
Q19: Courses with clear, practical assignments were easier to engage with because I knew exactly what was expected. Flexible deadlines were a double-edged sword—helpful when life got chaotic, but they also let me procrastinate more. Anything that required too much reading without hands-on application was harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. Good course design acknowledged that students have lives outside the LMS.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking through materials without really engaging, trying to feel productive without actually completing anything. It might also be someone searching for understanding but not finding it, so they keep returning. High activity doesn't mean high comprehension or completion. Sometimes activity is just anxious movement, not meaningful progress.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was lower, because I studied a lot offline. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need as much LMS time. Or they might have strong backgrounds that let them succeed with less visible effort. The LMS only captures one type of engagement, and some people's real learning happens elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you show up. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see if you're anxious-scrolling or genuinely learning. They miss offline study, emotional state, life circumstances, learning that happens in your head while you're on the bus. They capture behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm curious and want to figure something out, not just checking boxes. For me, real engagement is physical—doing practice problems, discussing with peers, applying concepts. It's mental presence, not just logged time. Unfortunately, my engagement was often survival-focused rather than curiosity-driven this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety shaped every interaction I had with the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying printed materials or the mental energy spent just managing my stress enough to function. It wouldn't show that I'm a 29-year-old with real-world pressures that younger students might not face. My engagement was constant but often invisible—worrying about the course counts as engagement in my experience, even if it's not productive.
